main_summary.summary: Bill Russell, interviewed in his home by Taylor Branch on May 13, 2013, for the Library of Congress and the National Museum of African American History and Culture. Russell traces his family's path from West Monroe, Louisiana, to Detroit, then Oakland, after his father Charles "Mr. Charlie" Russell left a foundry job over a refused raise and racial slur. He recounts his grandfather facing down the Ku Klux Klan with a shotgun loaded with birdshot, and the family's purchase of lumber to build a Black schoolhouse. Russell describes his mother Katie's death when he was twelve, his father's promise to raise the boys himself, and his middle name Felton after Southern University president Felton G. Clark. The bulk of the interview covers his basketball career: McClymonds High School, University of San Francisco under coach Phil Woolpert (1952-1956, including back-to-back NCAA championships and a 55-game win streak), the 1956 Olympic gold medal, and thirteen seasons with the Boston Celtics including eleven championships and his 1966 hire as the NBA's first Black head coach. Russell discusses Red Auerbach, Walter A. Brown, teammates Bob Cousy, Sam Jones, Tom Heinsohn, K.C. Jones, Frank Ramsey, John Havlicek, and Tom "Satch" Sanders. He addresses his trip to Jackson, Mississippi, in 1964 to support Charles Evers after Medgar Evers's assassination, the Cleveland summit supporting Muhammad Ali's draft refusal organized by Jim Brown, his pall-bearer role at Jackie Robinson's funeral, his autograph policy, his refusal to engage with the Red Sox until they joined his Mentoring USA board, and the Boston statue dedicated in 2013.
key_themes: Family migration from Jim Crow Louisiana to wartime Oakland
Basketball career arc: USF, Olympics, Boston Celtics
Race relations in Boston and integration of the NBA
Mentoring philosophy and Mentoring USA foundation
Solidarity with Muhammad Ali, Jackie Robinson, and the civil rights movement
main_summary.historical_significance: Russell provides primary-source testimony on multiple integration firsts in American sports: the Celtics drafting Chuck Cooper (first Black NBA draftee, 1950), drafting Sam Jones from a historically Black college in the first round, fielding the first all-Black starting five, and hiring Russell himself as the first Black head coach in any major American professional sport in 1966. He documents his family's experience of the Great Migration in personal detail (his father's confrontation with a foundry boss over a raise refused on racial grounds, the draft-board retaliation, the move to Detroit then Oakland's shipyards). The interview records his June 1964 trip to Jackson, Mississippi, to run integrated basketball clinics for Charles Evers a year after Medgar Evers's assassination, his participation in the 1967 Cleveland summit organized by Jim Brown supporting Muhammad Ali's stand on the Vietnam draft, his presence at the 1963 March on Washington, and his service as the only non-Brooklyn Dodger pall-bearer at Jackie Robinson's funeral. Russell's recollections of teammates and Red Auerbach provide on-the-record evidence of how a single franchise — under owner Walter A. Brown — moved against the racial conventions of mid-century professional sports.

chapters.title: Family Origins and Mr. Charlie
chapters.summary: Russell describes his parents Katie and Charles "Mr. Charlie" Russell, his grandfather's confrontation with the Ku Klux Klan in Louisiana using a shotgun loaded with birdshot, and the family's purchase of lumber to build a Black schoolhouse in defiance of a white lumber-yard owner. He recounts his mother's death from kidney failure when he was twelve and his father's promise to raise the boys himself, taking a foundry job at lower pay so he could be home each night.
related_events: Ku Klux Klan night attack in rural Louisiana

chapters.title: Great Migration to Oakland
chapters.summary: Russell describes his father's decision to leave Louisiana after a foundry boss refused him a raise and used a racial slur, the draft-board retaliation that followed, the move first to Detroit's Ford plants and then to Oakland's wartime shipyards, and his father's subsequent trucking business hauling fruit pickers to the Central Valley. He notes that he bought his own school clothes by picking fruit from age twelve through college.

chapters.title: USF and the Vertical Game
chapters.summary: Russell describes being cut from the USF junior varsity, being recruited to the varsity by coach Phil Woolpert, losing his athletic scholarship after a difficult freshman year and receiving a business-school academic scholarship instead, and how four of his teammates told the coach they did not appreciate playing with Black players. He describes inventing the vertical defensive game and shot-blocking, leading USF to a 55-game win streak and back-to-back NCAA championships in 1955 and 1956. He recounts the McClymonds High coach who taught him not to fight on the basketball court because "if Black kids fight, it's a riot."

chapters.title: Olympics and Joining the Celtics
chapters.summary: Russell describes the 1956 Olympic gold medal in Melbourne (beating the Soviet Union by 35 points), the 13-month run from college championship to Olympic gold to NBA championship in his rookie year. He recounts being met at the Boston airport by owner Walter A. Brown, Red Auerbach, and Bill Sharman, and the franchise's early integration milestones: Chuck Cooper from Duquesne (the first Black NBA draftee, 1950), the first all-Black starting five, and Sam Jones drafted in the first round from a historically Black college.

chapters.title: First Black NBA Head Coach
chapters.summary: Russell describes Red Auerbach's promise to hire no successor without Russell's full approval, the lists they exchanged with no overlap, and Auerbach offering Russell the player-coach role in 1966. He explains why he refused to take an assistant coach (the press would frame any assistant as the "real" coach) and how Auerbach's tradition of fighting referees to defend his players shaped Russell's own approach. He references Red Auerbach being thrown out of 22 games his last year as coach.

chapters.title: Teammates and the Celtic Way
chapters.summary: Russell sketches his teammates: Bill Sharman as the most thoughtful player; Bob Cousy as a poor defender whom Russell covered by inviting drives into the lane; Sam Jones who could have been all-time great but consciously dialed back his effort; Tom Heinsohn whom Auerbach pulled at six minutes regardless of performance; Frank Ramsey who pioneered the sixth-man role; and John Havlicek who initially passed up open shots before adjusting to the pro game. He describes the Celtics' practice of icing Havlicek's shoulder after games and the team philosophy that "kindness is an act of strength."

chapters.title: Boston, Race, and Walter A. Brown
chapters.summary: Russell describes the racial climate in Boston: sportswriters who would not book him for speaking engagements, a Herald reporter questioning his coaching the morning after a championship parade, and his decision to retire in 1969 without telling anyone so as not to overshadow Sam Jones's retirement parade. He recounts Walter A. Brown calling him in for a candid acknowledgement that the Celtics could not afford to pay him what he was worth, leading to an eight-year no-cut contract from Auerbach after Brown's death, which Russell did not collect because he could not take money he had not earned.

chapters.title: Philosophy of Sport and Other People's Kids
chapters.summary: Russell discusses his philosophical approach: "It is far more important to understand than to be understood." He describes a Texas speech where he argued for redirecting Vietnam War spending into education, met by a man asking why he should pay taxes for other people's kids. Russell's reply — that there are no other people's kids, only the next generation of Americans — became a recurring theme. He describes his role-model encounter with Minneapolis Lakers center George Mikan, who treated him with respect at age fourteen.
related_events: Vietnam War-era speech in Fort Worth

chapters.title: Civil Rights: Mississippi, Ali, March on Washington
chapters.summary: Russell describes his June 1964 trip to Jackson, Mississippi (during Freedom Summer when Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner were murdered) to run integrated basketball clinics at Charles Evers's request after Medgar Evers's assassination, with armed but invisible security. He recounts meeting Dr. King at the 1963 March on Washington and declining an invitation to the stage because he had not done the years of organizing work. He describes joining the 1967 Cleveland summit organized by Jim Brown to support Muhammad Ali's draft refusal after Ali's classification was arbitrarily changed to 1-A, and refusing a Boston demand to "disavow Muhammad Ali."

chapters.title: Autograph Policy and the Red Sox
chapters.summary: Russell describes his refusal to sign autographs as a matter of policy dating from 1964, his preference for conversation with kids instead, and his teammates Heinsohn, Ramsey, Sanders, and sometimes K.C. Jones forging his signature for fans. He recounts his refusal to throw out the Red Sox's first pitch and his demand that the team owners join his Mentoring USA board before he would reconcile. He describes the eventual outcome: an owner joined the board, the Red Sox host twice-yearly Mentoring nights at Fenway Park, and Russell threw out a first pitch about six years before the 2013 interview.

chapters.title: Presidential Medal and the Boston Statue
chapters.summary: Russell describes receiving the Presidential Medal of Freedom from Barack Obama at the dedication of the Martin Luther King monument in Washington (Obama called him "the reason I can be President"), and ranks it as a strong second to his father at age seventy-five telling him "this is my hero." He recounts Mayor Tom Menino's request to dedicate a statue in Boston at City Hall Plaza, his initial resistance ("it sounds a lot like a tombstone"), and Menino's argument that it would be for the city's community and racial relations. He recounts the legacy foundation and Mentoring fun-runs around the statue.

chapters.title: Jackie Robinson and Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: Russell describes Rachel Robinson asking him to serve as the only non-Brooklyn Dodger pall-bearer at Jackie Robinson's funeral, ranking this third in personal honors. He explains his view that Robinson took Black athletes from point A to point B, and that Russell wanted to take them from B to C. He references his conversation with Nelson Mandela on the role of violence in anti-apartheid struggle, declines to compare players across eras ("I would never ask a player to play against ghosts"), and reflects on his refusal to return to West Monroe, Louisiana, after his mother's burial.
